Born Invisible

The Global Crisis of Unregistered Children & Unsafe Water

Author

Omer Shahid

Published

April 14, 2026

⚙️ Load libraries & prepare data 
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
from plotnine import *
import warnings, importlib.util, os
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# ── Load data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ind1 = pd.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")   # Safe water access
ind2 = pd.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")   # Birth registration
meta = pd.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")

# ── Portable world shapefile ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
def _load_world():
    spec = importlib.util.find_spec("pyogrio")
    if spec:
        base = os.path.dirname(spec.origin)
        shp = os.path.join(base, "tests", "fixtures",
                           "naturalearth_lowres", "naturalearth_lowres.shp")
        if os.path.exists(shp):
            return gpd.read_file(shp)
    try:
        import geopandas.datasets
        return gpd.read_file(gpd.datasets.get_path("naturalearth_lowres"))
    except Exception:
        pass
    try:
        from geodatasets import get_path
        return gpd.read_file(get_path("naturalearth.land"))
    except Exception:
        pass
    raise RuntimeError("Could not load world shapefile.")

world = _load_world()

# ── Earthy/warm colour palette — distinct from UNICEF blue ────────────────────
C_DARK    = "#2C3E50"   # dark navy
C_RED     = "#C0392B"   # deep red
C_ORANGE  = "#E67E22"   # orange
C_GOLD    = "#F39C12"   # gold
C_GREEN   = "#27AE60"   # green
C_TEAL    = "#16A085"   # teal
C_SAND    = "#F5F0E8"   # warm sand background
C_GREY    = "#7F8C8D"   # grey
BLACK     = "#2C3E50"

# ── Tidy birth registration ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ind2.columns = ind2.columns.str.strip()
br_total  = ind2[ind2["sex"] == "Total"].copy()
br_latest = (br_total.sort_values("time_period", ascending=False)
                     .drop_duplicates("alpha_3_code")
                     .reset_index(drop=True))

# ── Tidy safe water ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ind1.columns = ind1.columns.str.strip()
sw_latest = (ind1.sort_values("time_period", ascending=False)
                 .drop_duplicates("alpha_3_code")
                 .reset_index(drop=True))

# ── Merge for scatter — include population for bubble sizing ──────────────────
meta_pop = (meta.dropna(subset=["Population, total"])
                .sort_values("year", ascending=False)
                .drop_duplicates("alpha_3_code")
            [["alpha_3_code", "Population, total"]]
            .rename(columns={"Population, total": "population"}))

scatter_df = (br_latest[["alpha_3_code", "country", "obs_value"]]
              .merge(sw_latest[["alpha_3_code", "obs_value"]]
                     .rename(columns={"obs_value": "water_access"}),
                     on="alpha_3_code", how="inner")
              .rename(columns={"obs_value": "birth_reg"})
              .merge(meta_pop, on="alpha_3_code", how="left"))
scatter_df["pop_m"] = scatter_df["population"] / 1e6  # millions

# ── Time series ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ts_countries = ["Bangladesh", "Pakistan", "Afghanistan",
                "Nigeria", "Ethiopia", "Chad"]
ts_df = ind1[ind1["country"].isin(ts_countries)].copy()

“A child without birth registration is invisible to the state — without identity, access to education, healthcare, and legal protection becomes uncertain.”

The global crisis of unregistered children runs parallel to another silent emergency: lack of access to safe drinking water. Both crises share the same root cause — weak institutions and failing public services. This report uses UNICEF data to expose where these failures are most severe, and what the numbers mean for real children.

The Numbers That Shock

1 in 3 children in Sub-Saharan Africa are born without legal identity

2.7% Ethiopia’s birth registration rate — the world’s lowest

1 in 5 children globally lack safely managed drinking water

Part I — The Geography of Invisibility

Where are unregistered children most concentrated? The map below reveals a stark pattern — the darker the shade, the lower the birth registration rate.

🗺️ World map — birth registration rate by country 
map_br = world.merge(
    br_latest[["alpha_3_code", "obs_value"]],
    left_on="iso_a3", right_on="alpha_3_code", how="left"
)

# Invert: low registration = dark red, high = light sand
(
    ggplot(map_br)
    + geom_map(aes(fill="obs_value"), color="#FFFFFF", size=0.2)
    + scale_fill_gradient(
        low=C_RED, high="#D5E8D4",
        name="Registered\nChildren (%)",
        na_value="#BDC3C7",
        limits=(0, 100)
    )
    + labs(
        title="Birth Registration Rate by Country",
        subtitle="Deep red = fewer registered children | Light green = near-universal registration | Grey = no data",
        caption="Source: UNICEF  |  Latest available year per country"
    )
    + theme_void()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(12, 5.5),
        plot_title=element_text(size=16, weight="bold", color=C_DARK, margin={"b": 5}),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9, color=C_GREY, margin={"b": 8}),
        plot_caption=element_text(size=7, color=C_GREY),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill=C_SAND),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill=C_SAND),
        legend_title=element_text(size=8),
    )
)
Warning📍 Key Insight

Countries with the lowest birth registration rates cluster in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Weak administrative systems, poverty, and limited public services leave millions of children born without any legal identity. Ethiopia (2.7%), Somalia (3%) and Papua New Guinea (13%) are among the most critical cases.

Part II — The 15 Nations Most Failing Their Children

Every bar below represents a country where the majority of children grow up without a birth certificate — and without the rights and protections that come with one.

📊 Bar chart — countries with lowest birth registration 
bottom15 = (
    br_latest.nsmallest(15, "obs_value")
             .sort_values("obs_value")
             .assign(country=lambda d: pd.Categorical(
                 d["country"], categories=d["country"], ordered=True))
)
# Assign colour bands
bottom15["band"] = pd.cut(
    bottom15["obs_value"],
    bins=[-1, 10, 25, 50],
    labels=["Critical (<10%)", "Severe (10–25%)", "High (25–50%)"]
)

band_colors = {
    "Critical (<10%)":   C_RED,
    "Severe (10–25%)":   C_ORANGE,
    "High (25–50%)":     C_GOLD,
}

(
    ggplot(bottom15, aes(x="country", y="obs_value", fill="band"))
    + geom_col(width=0.7)
    + geom_text(aes(label="obs_value.round(1)"),
                ha="left", nudge_y=0.4, size=8, color=BLACK)
    + scale_fill_manual(values=band_colors, name="Severity")
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=(0, 52), expand=(0, 0))
    + labs(
        title="Countries with the Lowest Birth Registration Rates",
        subtitle="% of children under age 5 whose births are officially registered — latest available year",
        x=None,
        y="Birth Registration Rate (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Indicator Data"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(11, 6),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color=C_DARK),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9, color=C_GREY),
        plot_caption=element_text(size=7, color=C_GREY),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill=C_SAND),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill=C_SAND),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        legend_position="right",
        axis_text=element_text(size=9),
    )
)
Important🚨 These Are Not Just Statistics

Ethiopia records just 2.7% — meaning 97 in every 100 children are born with no legal proof of existence. Somalia (3%), Papua New Guinea (13.4%), and Zambia (14%) follow. Without a birth certificate, a child cannot enrol in school, access healthcare, own property, or vote. Invisibility is not just a statistic — it is a life sentence.

Part III — Safe Water: Progress That Is Too Slow

Tracking safe water access from 2000 to 2024 across six of the most affected countries reveals that progress — while real — is deeply uneven.

📈 Time-series — safe water access 2000–2024 
country_colors = {
    "Bangladesh":  C_TEAL,
    "Pakistan":    C_GREEN,
    "Afghanistan": C_ORANGE,
    "Nigeria":     C_GOLD,
    "Ethiopia":    C_RED,
    "Chad":        C_DARK,
}

# Add end-of-line labels
ts_end = ts_df.sort_values("time_period").groupby("country").last().reset_index()

(
    ggplot(ts_df, aes(x="time_period", y="obs_value",
                      color="country", group="country"))
    + geom_line(size=1.4, alpha=0.9)
    + geom_point(size=2.2, alpha=0.8)
    + geom_text(data=ts_end,
                mapping=aes(x="time_period", y="obs_value", label="country", color="country"),
                nudge_x=0.5, ha="left", size=8)
    + scale_color_manual(values=country_colors, guide=None)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2000, 2025, 2)),
                         limits=(2000, 2027))
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=(0, 70),
                         labels=lambda l: [f"{int(v)}%" for v in l])
    + labs(
        title="Progress in Safe Water Access, 2000–2024",
        subtitle="% of population using safely managed drinking water services",
        x=None,
        y="Safe Water Access",
        caption="Source: UNICEF  |  Indicator: Safely managed drinking water services"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(12, 6),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color=C_DARK),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9, color=C_GREY),
        plot_caption=element_text(size=7, color=C_GREY),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill=C_SAND),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill=C_SAND),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_text_x=element_text(angle=35),
        legend_position="none",
    )
)
Note📊 Trend Insight

Bangladesh leads the group, rising steadily from under 20% to nearly 60% safe water access. Chad and Ethiopia remain trapped below 15% even in 2024 — barely any progress over 24 years. Afghanistan and Nigeria show gains but remain critically low. Countries with weak water access are almost always the same ones with the lowest birth registration — these crises are inseparable.

Part IV — Two Crises, One Root Cause

This chart plots every country by its safe water access rate (X axis) against its birth registration rate (Y axis). Each bubble is one country — bigger bubbles represent larger populations.

🔵 Bubble chart — birth registration vs safe water access 
label_countries = [
    "Ethiopia", "Chad", "Mozambique", "Uganda", "Nigeria", "Afghanistan",
    "Bangladesh", "Pakistan", "Zimbabwe", "Ghana", "Nepal", "Madagascar",
    "Kiribati", "Nauru", "Fiji", "India", "Paraguay", "Samoa",
    "South Africa", "Ecuador", "Zambia", "Somalia"
]
scatter_df["label"] = scatter_df["country"].apply(
    lambda c: c if c in label_countries else ""
)
# Cap pop for sizing
scatter_df["pop_sz"] = scatter_df["pop_m"].clip(upper=500).fillna(50)

(
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="water_access", y="birth_reg"))
    + geom_point(aes(size="pop_sz", fill="birth_reg"),
                 color="white", alpha=0.75, shape="o")
    + geom_text(aes(label="label"), size=7,
                nudge_y=3, color=C_DARK, ha="center")
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color=C_GREY, se=False,
                  linetype="dashed", size=1)
    + scale_fill_gradient(low=C_RED, high=C_GREEN,
                          name="Birth Reg\nRate (%)")
    + scale_size_continuous(range=(2, 14), guide=None)
    + scale_x_continuous(limits=(0, 105),
                         breaks=list(range(0, 101, 10)),
                         labels=lambda l: [f"{int(v)}%" for v in l])
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=(0, 110),
                         breaks=list(range(0, 101, 20)),
                         labels=lambda l: [f"{int(v)}%" for v in l])
    + labs(
        title="Birth Registration vs Safe Water Access by Country",
        subtitle="Bubble size = population  |  Colour = birth registration rate  |  Dashed line = trend",
        x="Safe Water Access (%)",
        y="Birth Registration Rate (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF  |  Each bubble = one country  |  Latest available year"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(12, 7),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color=C_DARK),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9, color=C_GREY),
        plot_caption=element_text(size=7, color=C_GREY),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill=C_SAND),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill=C_SAND),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_text=element_text(size=9),
    )
)
Note🔍 Relationship Insight

The dashed trend line confirms a strong positive relationship — countries with better safe water access consistently show higher birth registration rates. Both indicators are driven by the same underlying force: institutional capacity. Countries in the bottom-left corner (low water, low registration) are almost entirely in Sub-Saharan Africa. The top-right cluster (high water, high registration) is dominated by Europe and East Asia.

Conclusion

Birth registration and access to safe drinking water are both fundamental indicators of development. Countries that lack essential infrastructure often struggle on both fronts simultaneously — and the children who suffer most are the ones who are never counted.

The path forward requires:

  1. 📋 Universal birth registration — free, automatic, and accessible at every health facility
  2. 💧 Investment in water infrastructure — particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa and fragile states
  3. 📱 Digital civil registration — mobile-based systems to reach remote communities
  4. 📊 Consistent data collection — countries cannot fix what they cannot measure

“Strengthening public services, improving governance, and investing in basic infrastructure are critical steps toward ensuring that every child is counted, protected, and given a legal identity.”

Data Sources

File Indicator
unicef_indicator_1.csv % of population using safely managed drinking water
unicef_indicator_2.csv % of children under age 5 whose births are registered
unicef_metadata.csv GDP, population, life expectancy (World Bank via UNICEF)
Natural Earth World map geometries

Visualisations built with Python (plotnine, geopandas) and rendered with Quarto.

UNICEF — For Every Child
Every child deserves an identity. Every child deserves clean water.
